results.title: What’s holding back physical AI from real-world performance | Deloitte Insights
results.content: know, practice to get it to fold laundry, something like that. But then as soon as you move the camera or change one thing about the scenario, it all breaks. So, they're very brittle, and they also can't deal with forces and weights. It's all lightweight stuff that's basically position trajectory. So, there's a huge limitation to the approaches people are taking there right now. The next layer up, the cognitive intelligence with the language models or vision models— that's the thing that has surprised everybody and is, fortunately, a rising tide that lifts all boats because it's very general. It applies to just any robot that you're using, and it's what brings human context and reasoning into these machines to operate in more general environments. So, I think the real challenge is in this [...] that, you can't tell if it's the hardware that is limiting it, or if there is their AI is not very good, or you don't know what. But, for a lot of them, it really is just fundamentally limited by the hardware. It doesn't matter what AI they have. So, that's one foundation—is able-bodied hardware. The next layer up from that is control layers, and that is not data driven. Like a large language model, you have the whole internet of data to train on, right? For controlling a particular robot, there is no source of data. There is no example anywhere of what's the right set of motor torques for a set of sensor inputs on that robot, right. So it's necessary to explore it yourself, to discover the right solutions. And for that, a really good tool is reinforcement learning and simulation for a [...] environments. So, I think the real challenge is in this physical AI—the ability to learn skills, learn them well, and then figure out how to practice them for your particular robot so they get really, really good at it.

results.title: Why Physical AI is Hard | Dexterity
results.content: This is not one AI problem. It is dozens of AI problems that must be solved simultaneously and composed together into a coherent system. Perception, planning, motion control, force control, task allocation, collision avoidance, anomaly detection: each is a frontier research challenge in its own right. Solving them individually is hard. Making them work together reliably is where most approaches fail. [...] The core challenge is this: the physical world is infinitely variable. Objects are deformable. Environments change between shifts. Lighting conditions fluctuate. Boxes arrive in random sizes, weights, and orientations. A system that works perfectly in simulation will fail the moment it encounters a crushed box, a shifted pallet, or a trailer floor that is not perfectly flat. [...] The safety requirements compound the difficulty. Say a robot performs 300 actions per hour. To run for just one month without a single safety incident, you need 99.9995% confidence that every action is safe. Most people would prefer their robots run for ten years before breaking, and never injure a person. This level of reliability cannot be achieved by any single monolithic AI model. It requires an architecture where every component is interpretable, every action is bounded, and every failure mode is anticipated.

results.title: Robotics Deep Dive: How Physical AI Is Winning Without a ...
results.content:
Look for companies running production fleets with measurable uptime, not lab demos. Prioritize businesses where the RaaS model has proven unit economics and customers are renewing or expanding deployments. Focus on verticals where labor economics have fundamentally broken—warehouses running 24/7 to keep pace with e-commerce volatility, manufacturers losing workers faster than they can hire.

The foundation model companies and humanoid platforms may eventually prove transformative. But in 2026, the safer growth stage bet is on the operators who’ve already crossed the chasm from research to reliable revenue, and are using that traction to build moats that pure-play model builders can’t easily replicate. [...] Miso Robotics

Saman Farid, CEO of Formic (which employees the RaaS model for industrial robot arms for palletizing, machine tending, and assembly), described the same dynamic as a system-wide staffing breakdown: “Manufacturing faces a large and painful labor shortage.” One customer “hired more than 250 people in a single year just to keep ~100 roles filled.” The real cost is churn—”those 12 palletizing jobs are getting turned over four times a year… which is 60 people.”

Sviat Dulianov, CEO of Bright Machines (a software-defined robot that automates production of complex electronics, especially AI and data-center infrastructure) says: “the only scalable answer is to replace skill with software/AI and replace scale with robots that can run 24/7.” [...] The tradeoff is complexity and cost. Bipedal locomotion is energy-intensive and difficult to control. Two-armed manipulation increases failure modes. Battery life is constrained. While costs are declining with scale, humanoids remain expensive and operationally demanding. Most deployments today focus on narrow, repetitive tasks—tote handling, simple assembly—rather than general-purpose labor.

Industrial (Non Humanoid)

Industrial robots don’t resemble humans. They optimize for efficiency, reliability, and cost within structured environments.

results.title: Why physical AI is becoming manufacturing’s next advantage | MIT Technology Review
results.content:
This transition exposes a critical gap. Traditional automation excels at repetition but struggles with adaptability. Human workers bring judgment and context but are constrained by scale. Physical AI closes that gap by enabling human‑led, AI‑operated systems, where people set intent and intelligent systems execute, learn, and improve over time. Humans are essential for scaled success.

### Microsoft and NVIDIA: Accelerating physical AI at scale

Physical AI cannot be delivered through point solutions. It requires agentic-driven, enterprise-grade development, deployment, and operations toolchains and workflows that connect simulation, data, AI models, robotics, and governance into a coherent system. [...] NVIDIA is building the AI infrastructure that makes physical AI possible, including accelerated computing, open models, simulation libraries, and robotics frameworks and blueprints that enable the ecosystem to build autonomous robotics systems that can perceive, reason, plan, and take action in the physical world. Microsoft complements this with a cloud and data platform designed to operate physical AI securely, at scale, and across the enterprise.

Together, Microsoft and NVIDIA are enabling manufacturers to move beyond pilots toward production‑ready physical AI systems that can be developed, tested, deployed, and continuously improved across heterogeneous environments spanning the product lifecycle, factory operations, and supply chain. [...] Intelligence: AI systems must understand how the business actually handles its data, workflows, and institutional knowledge.
 Trust: As AI begins to act in high‑stakes environments, organizations must retain security, governance, and observability at every layer.

Without intelligence, AI becomes generic. Without trust, adoption stalls.

### Why manufacturing is the proving ground for physical AI

Manufacturing is uniquely positioned at the center of this shift.

AI is no longer confined to planning or analytics. It is moving into physical execution: coordinating machines, adapting to real‑world variability, and working alongside people on the factory floor. Robotics, autonomous systems, and AI agents must now perceive, reason, and act in dynamic environments.
